_meta.description: Cycle & innings — where each vertical sits in its structural growth cycle (1-9 innings), with structural-demand strength, capex under-investment, and business-cycle risk scored 0-100. Verdict is a quick read, not advice.

cycles.sum: The most defensible, least cyclical layer — royalty/license model that compounds with design starts. Well-invested (no supply gap). Main risk is valuation and the geopolitical on/off lever to China, not demand.

Structural beneficiary of every fab buildout, but WFE spend is lumpy and China-localization demand is rolling over. ASML and the deep subsystem chokepoints (Zeiss, VAT) are the durable holds; broad WFE is more cyclical.

Leading-edge demand is structural and supply is capital-constrained (years to add). TSMC compounds; mature-node faces China overcapacity. The keystone of the universe, carrying the Taiwan-concentration tail risk.

HBM is structurally tight and sold out, but memory is historically the most cyclical semi segment. The tension: HBM oligopoly pricing power vs the ever-present risk of a conventional-DRAM glut souring sentiment.

Value is migrating from front-end to back-end as scaling slows. CoWoS, substrates and bonding tools are under-built vs demand. Early-mid innings of a multi-year capacity-build with high structural support.

Highest structural demand in the universe, but also the highest scrutiny: the whole stack hinges on hyperscaler capex ROI. Custom ASICs add a second growth vector. Mid-innings with elevated cycle/ROI risk.

The network increasingly gates cluster utilization, and content per cluster is rising. Broadcom + connectivity silicon (Astera, Credo) are the picks-and-shovels. Early-mid; tied to the same capex cycle as compute.

Power and cooling are the binding constraints and badly under-built — liquid cooling is at an inflection. Early innings with very high structural support; the deepest cross-link to the energy supply chain.

Optics content per GPU is scaling fast and EML lasers are a genuine chokepoint. CPO (2026) is a step-change ahead. Early innings; main risk is technology transitions (CPO/LPO) reshuffling the value chain.

Big structural promise (a whole new BOM) but highly speculative on timing and volume. Component chokepoints (reducers, screws) are the cleanest exposure. Very early innings — size positions to the uncertainty.

Rides both robotics and broader automation. Sony sensors and the Nvidia robot-brain are durable; force/torque and tactile sensing are an unsolved gap. Early innings with steadier non-humanoid demand underneath.

A make-or-break AR component with no clear winning technology yet — high risk, high optionality. Most leaders are private (JBD). Very early; the bet is on which display tech (microLED/OLEDoS/LCoS) scales.

Earliest-stage theme in the universe — full AR is years out and waveguide tech is unsettled. Meta's Ray-Ban breakout is the consumer on-ramp. Very early; component layer (glass, SoC) is more investable than device brands.
